channel.title: Hetzner on Ramblings about all things technical
channel.description: Recent content in Hetzner on Ramblings about all things technical

item.title: Template rendering and secrets management with Plato
item.description: The problem with secrets If you’ve been following my blog you might have noticed a recurring theme throughout my posts, and that is I’m obsessed with automation and keeping everything as code in git repositories. My entire Kubernetes infrastructure is defined in code, deployed automatically via GitHub Actions, and reproducible from scratch. But there’s always been one annoying thorn in my side: secrets. 😤 I wrote about using Mozilla/SOPS for secret management a while back, and while SOPS itself is a fantastic tool for encrypting secrets so they can safely live in a git repository, the way I was using those secrets in my deployment pipelines was… let’s just say it was not pretty.

item.title: Back to K3s ???
item.description: I did it again -heavy sigh- So remember how I deployed a proper HA Kubernetes cluster with KubeOne? Three control plane nodes, two workers, load balancer in front, the whole production-grade setup? Yeah, I tore it all down. 😂 I’m back on a single-node K3s cluster on Hetzner Cloud, just like before. And honestly? It’s just better for my use-case. Let me explain why. The HA experiment Don’t get me wrong, KubeOne is a fantastic tool.

item.title: Deploying Kubernetes with KubeOne
item.description: My Kubernetes journey so far If you’ve been reading this blog you know I’ve been through quite the odyssey when it comes to running Kubernetes. It started with a managed K8s on Scaleway, then I got fed up with the lack of control and built my own personal K3s cluster on Hetzner Cloud, later automated the whole thing with Terraform on vCloud Director, and most recently started using Plato for templating and secret management.

item.title: My own personal Kubernetes
item.description: Managed Kubernetes? On my journey where I was (unfortunately) forced to migrate from Cloud Foundry to Kubernetes, I first went with a “Managed Kubernetes-as-a-Service” offering. You can read about that here. But it didn’t take long for problems and issues to start emerging. The cluster not being on exactly the version I wanted it to be, pre-installed components that didn’t behave they way I wanted (nginx ingress controller for example), price hikes and occasional outages, not really a lot of insight into the behind the scenes of my cluster, etc.
